TranslationContext: This file contains Unit Names for the Abyssal Legions Faction.

Translations.Comment: Organized demonic armies. Used plural 'Legioni' with 'Abissali' adjective to match Spanish pattern and emphasize corruption from the abyss.
Northern warriors. Used 'Nordici' for warriors and 'dell'Ascia' (of the axe) to emphasize barbarian identity and weapon specialization.
Core infantry unit. 'Guerrieri' with 'Abissali' expresses the demonic corruption, consistent with previous faction patterns.
Corrupted dragon-like creatures. Used 'Cria' (spawn/brood) paralleling Spanish 'Cría' to convey bestial nature and dragon heritage.
Northern cavalry. Masculine 'Cavalieri' emphasizes mounted warriors from the barbaric north, parallel to Spanish 'Jinetes Nórdicos'.
Corrupted knights. Heavy demonic cavalry unit. 'Cavalieri Abissali' maintains nobility theme while showing corruption.
War hounds. Used 'Mastini' (mastiff-type hounds) for martial ferocity; 'Abissali' shows corruption, matching Spanish 'Sabuesos'.
Demonic war machine. Direct compound emphasizes brutal speed and infernal engineering, parallel to Spanish 'Carro Abisal'.
Greater demon lord. Maintains consistency with Abyssal Demons faction translation, conveying supreme demonic authority.
Lesser demons. Maintains consistency with Abyssal Demons faction translation, emphasizing infernal breeding.
Corrupted mammoth. Direct translation emphasizing massive, unstoppable nature of the corrupted beast.
Premier flying threat. Conveys terrifying power and ancient corruption, parallel to Spanish 'Dragón Abisal'.
Mythological beast. Standard Italian term for manticore, maintaining the creature's fearsome and ancient nature.
Elite hero unit. Singular form emphasizes individual might and demonic mastery, parallel to Spanish 'Campeón Abisal'.
Dark spellcaster. Used 'Stregone' to convey arcane corruption and malevolent intent in the abyssal service.
Barbarian warlord. Used 'Capo Branco' (pack leader) to emphasize brutal, animalistic leadership of corrupted northern warriors.
